speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're exploring a side of GitHub that matters just as much as commits and pull requests: how people present themselves, support each other, organize communities, and discover projects. GitHub is not only a code host. It's also a social and collaborative space.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that framing, because a lot of learners think, I need to master Git commands first, and only then maybe worry about profile pages or stars or organizations.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, but these features are part of real contribution workflows. They affect how you find projects, how maintainers communicate, how documentation is shared, and how other people understand who you are.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with three community-facing features: your profile README, GitHub Sponsors, and repository wikis. Think of them as three different kinds of presence. Your profile README is how people meet you. Sponsors is how people financially support open source work. Wikis are one way projects share extra documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are accessibility details built into all three, which is especially important for this audience.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. For screen reader users, a profile README is read like regular page content on your profile, so heading structure matters. The Sponsor control is a standard button, so button navigation can help you find it quickly. And wiki pages behave like other GitHub Markdown pages, so heading navigation is useful there too.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about low vision learners?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few practical notes. Profile READMEs respect GitHub's light and dark themes, so test readability in both. The Sponsor button uses a heart icon with a pink or magenta accent, usually near the profile photo or repository header. And wiki navigation often appears in a sidebar area with links to pages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for sighted users, the profile README is basically right below your avatar and bio, the Sponsor button sits near Star and Watch, and wiki pages usually show a page list in the sidebar.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now, profile customization. GitHub has a special feature: if you create a public repository whose name exactly matches your username, and the owner is you, then that repository's README appears on your profile page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if my username were jamie-prof, I'd create a repository also named jamie-prof?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Same spelling, same case, owned by your account. That README becomes your profile README.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's such a wonderfully GitHub-ish trick.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. To create it, you make a new repository, name it exactly as your username, make it public, initialize it with a README, and then edit that README with the introduction you want visitors to see.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should actually go in it?

speaker: ALEX
text: A simple structure works well. Start with an introduction: who you are and what you work on. Then your current focus, like technologies or projects you're learning. You can add skills if that's helpful, though it is optional. Include ways to reach you, such as email, LinkedIn, or a personal site. And if you want, a fun fact or human detail.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not a giant résumé. More like a welcoming front door.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. For example, someone might say they are an accessibility advocate and open source contributor, then list a current focus like NVDA documentation, accessible React components, or learning TypeScript. Then they might mention skills such as JavaScript, Python, HTML and CSS, screen reader testing, Git, GitHub, and Actions. Then a contact section, then perhaps a small personal note.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That example works because it tells me what kind of work they care about, not just what tools they've touched.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and that's part of the best-practice advice. Keep it concise because visitors skim. Update it occasionally so it does not feel abandoned. Be authentic instead of stuffing it with buzzwords. Include links so people can continue the conversation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And also, please, maybe don't turn it into a carnival of badges.

speaker: ALEX
text: Please don't. Excessive badges create visual clutter and screen reader noise. Auto-generated stats widgets can also be inaccessible or distracting. A short, thoughtful README is usually better than an elaborate one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's slow down on accessibility here, because this is one of those places where people accidentally make their profile less usable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Use headings like level two headings for major sections. Provide alt text for images. Avoid ASCII art, because screen readers read it character by character, which is tedious. And if possible, test your profile README with a screen reader before publishing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone wants flair, they should choose flair that still respects navigation and readability.

speaker: ALEX
text: Precisely.

speaker: ALEX
text: There are also other profile customizations worth knowing. You can pin up to six repositories on your profile. That's useful for highlighting your best work, beginner-friendly projects, or things that represent your interests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that's through the profile interface where you customize your pins?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You choose which repositories appear first. Then there's the contribution graph, which shows activity over the past year. Commits, pull requests, issues, and related activity can all contribute to that graph.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Important note: you can't manually redesign the graph.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. It reflects activity rather than being something you style. And you can also set a temporary status message from your profile, like being away, focusing, or available for collaboration.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds small, but status messages can be helpful socially. They tell people whether now is a good time to expect a reply.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's move to GitHub Sponsors. GitHub Sponsors is a way to financially support developers and projects you depend on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I rely on an open source maintainer's work every week, Sponsors gives me a built-in way to support them?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A developer or project can create a Sponsors profile, define tiers, and receive recurring support. The learner-friendly way to think about it is: Patreon-style support for open source, integrated into GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does it work at a high level?

speaker: ALEX
text: Maintainers create a Sponsors profile. You choose a monthly tier, maybe five dollars, ten dollars, twenty-five dollars, or a custom amount. Payment goes to the developer, and the platform is designed so GitHub does not take fees from that sponsorship flow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why should someone sponsor instead of just starring a repository and saying thank you?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because stars are appreciation and visibility, but sponsorship supports sustainability. Many maintainers are doing critical work in their free time. Sponsorship can help them keep a project alive. It's also a direct form of gratitude. And sometimes maintainers offer sponsor benefits like private chats, early releases, or priority support.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you actually sponsor someone?

speaker: ALEX
text: Go to a user or repository page, find the Sponsor button with the heart icon, choose a tier or amount, select a payment method such as card or PayPal, and complete the process. GitHub sends a receipt, and your sponsorship may appear publicly on your profile if you choose that visibility.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Accessibility tip: for screen reader users, that Sponsor control is a button, so pressing the shortcut to move through buttons can help you locate it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It's often near the profile photo area or near the repository title and action buttons.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And can learners receive sponsorships too, or is that only for famous maintainers?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can absolutely apply to receive sponsorships if you maintain projects or contribute regularly. The basic setup is to go to the Sponsors page, join or set up your profile, connect a payment method like Stripe or a bank account, create sponsorship tiers with descriptions, and then share that page with your audience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I appreciate that the source specifically notes accessibility advocates often use Sponsors to fund work on assistive technology and inclusive design.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That's an important reminder that accessibility work is real technical labor and worth supporting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now, GitHub Wikis. A wiki is a documentation space attached to a repository but separate from the main code tree.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not the README, and not GitHub Pages either.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. It's a lightweight, Markdown-based place for multi-page documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When is a wiki a good fit?

speaker: ALEX
text: When you need multi-page docs such as tutorials, guides, FAQs, or community notes, especially if the material is too long or too specific for the README. It's also useful when you want a more community-editable documentation area.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when is it not a good fit?

speaker: ALEX
text: If your project already has a full docs site, like GitHub Pages or an external documentation platform. Also if documentation must be version-controlled tightly with the source code, because the wiki is actually a separate Git repository. And if you need deep customization, the wiki is more limited than a full site.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That separate repository detail matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much. To access a wiki, you go to the repository and open the Wiki tab. If none exists, you may be offered a way to create the first page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And making pages is pretty straightforward?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Open the Wiki tab, choose New page, add a title and Markdown content, then save. The wiki automatically includes navigation, usually through a sidebar listing pages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should learners know about wiki accessibility?

speaker: ALEX
text: The editor is similar to the issue and pull request comment editor, so familiar Markdown features work: headings, lists, links, code blocks. Use a proper heading hierarchy for navigation. You can link between wiki pages using wiki link syntax with double brackets around a page title.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's an especially important caveat here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Because the wiki is a separate Git repository, changes pushed directly to the wiki remote are not governed by the main repository's branch protection rules. That means the normal pull request review safeguards may not apply. So treat wikis as supplementary, community-editable docs, not your primary source for critical reviewed documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is one of those details contributors really need to understand before assuming every GitHub surface has the same review workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: From there, let's widen out to organizations, templates, and repository settings. These are structural features. They shape how projects are owned, duplicated, and presented to contributors.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So now we're talking less about your personal profile and more about the architecture around repositories.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's right. Start with repository templates. A template repository is a starter repository intended to be used as the basis for new repositories.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How is that different from a fork?

speaker: ALEX
text: A fork preserves a relationship to the original repository. It's meant for contributing back or maintaining your own related copy. A template creates a new repository from the starting files without creating that fork relationship. So if you're bootstrapping a fresh project, template. If you're building on someone else's project history and likely collaborating upstream, fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That distinction helps. Template for starting point, fork for lineage.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Creating a template repository usually means marking an existing repository as a template in repository settings. Using a template typically means choosing the option to create a new repository from that template, then deciding things like the new name, owner, and whether to include all branches or only the default branch depending on the interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users, the path matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Usually you're on the template repository page and activate the control to use this template, then follow the repository creation form. The exact wording may vary over time, but conceptually that's the path.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now organizations. A GitHub organization is an account type used by teams, companies, nonprofits, and many open source communities.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of repositories belonging to one individual, they belong to a shared organization account.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. That's why many mature projects live under organization names. Organizations support multiple owners, repositories, teams, permissions, and policies.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does joining an organization usually look like from a contributor's perspective?

speaker: ALEX
text: You may be invited by email or through GitHub. Once you accept, you become a member. Depending on the organization, your membership might be public or private.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack membership visibility.

speaker: ALEX
text: Public membership means other people can see on your profile that you belong to that organization. Private membership hides that relationship from public view, though of course the organization itself still knows you're a member.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And teams inside organizations?

speaker: ALEX
text: Teams are subgroups used to manage permissions and communication. For example, docs team, accessibility team, maintainers team, release team. Contributors may interact with teams through mentions, access controls, and code ownership workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I'm navigating an organization profile page, what should I expect?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually an overview of the organization, repository listings, pinned repositories, people, sometimes packages or projects, and possibly links to teams if you have permission. For learners, the key idea is that the organization page is often the front door to a whole ecosystem rather than a single project.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next, repository settings that contributors should understand even if they cannot change them. First, visibility.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Public versus private?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Public repositories are visible to anyone. Private repositories are restricted. As a contributor, this affects discoverability, access, and whether your work is visible publicly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Archived repositories are another important signal.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. An archived repository is generally read-only and no longer actively maintained. If you find one, treat it as historical unless the maintainers clearly indicate otherwise.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Topics are also part of repository settings, and they matter more than people realize.

speaker: ALEX
text: They do. Topics are descriptive tags attached to repositories. They improve discoverability and help people find projects by category, such as accessibility, screen-reader, docs, education, python, or beginner-friendly themes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there's the default branch name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Contributors need to know which branch is the main starting point, because it affects where pull requests target and what branch you compare against. Not every repository uses the same default branch name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's dive into social features more broadly. Stars, watching, following, your home feed, Explore, Trending, topics, lists, and strategies for finding accessible and inclusive projects. These are not fluff. They are practical discovery and community tools.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with stars, because almost everyone sees that button early on.

speaker: ALEX
text: A star is basically a bookmark plus a public signal of appreciation. It lets you save a repository to your starred list, and it also contributes to visible interest around that project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why does star count matter to projects?

speaker: ALEX
text: It's not a perfect measure of quality, but it can indicate interest, trust, momentum, or discoverability. More stars can help a project show up in searches and encourage others to take a look.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you star a repository?

speaker: ALEX
text: On GitHub.com, go to the repository page and activate the Star button. If you use keyboard navigation, there may be a shortcut depending on context, though buttons and headings are usually more reliable navigation anchors. For screen reader users, button navigation works well to find the Star control.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are CLI options too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. With GitHub CLI, you can star a repository, unstar it, list your starred repositories, and check whether you've starred a repo. The exact command syntax depends on the CLI version, but conceptually those actions are available.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So stars are manageable from both the web and command line.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And viewing your stars on the website gives you a personal collection of interesting repositories.

speaker: ALEX
text: Watching a repository is different. Watching is about notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So starring says, I care about this project, while watching says, keep me updated.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfect distinction. Watching determines whether you receive notifications about activity in that repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are multiple watch levels, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Depending on GitHub's interface, you may watch all activity, watch only certain kinds of events like releases, or ignore the repository entirely. The web interface offers the most granular control.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Important technical note: some of those granular watch levels are really web-interface features, not something you fully manage through the API alone.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Through CLI, you can do core actions like watch all activity, ignore a repository, list watched repositories, or stop watching. But if you want a very specific notification mode, the web UI may be required.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a good example of not assuming the CLI exposes every nuance.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. As a learner, use watching deliberately. Watching too many active repositories can flood your notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So star broadly, watch selectively.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's often wise.

speaker: ALEX
text: Following people is the social equivalent for users. When you follow someone, their public activity can influence what you see and helps you keep track of their work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Who should learners consider following?

speaker: ALEX
text: Maintainers you learn from, accessibility advocates, tool builders, educators, organizations, and contributors whose issue discussions or pull requests teach you something.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you follow someone?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open their profile and activate Follow. Screen reader users can navigate by buttons to find that control. In CLI, you can also follow a user, unfollow a user, list who you're following, list your followers, check whether you follow a specific person, and inspect who another user follows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last one is a powerful discovery trick. Looking at who thoughtful people follow can lead you to more thoughtful people.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And when viewing someone's profile, pay attention not just to follower counts but to pinned repositories, README content, organizations, recent activity, and whether their work aligns with your interests.

speaker: ALEX
text: Your home feed is what you see when you log in. It includes activity from repositories and people you follow or interact with, plus recommendations and discovery items.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it is not just a dashboard. It's a discovery tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It helps you notice trends in your own circles. You may see releases, stars, discussions, pull requests, and repository activity. Over time, your feed reflects your interests and connections.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you customize it?

speaker: ALEX
text: To a degree, yes, mostly indirectly by what you follow, what you star, what you watch, and what organizations or repositories you engage with. The main lesson is that you can shape your feed by shaping your network.

speaker: ALEX
text: Explore is GitHub's broader discovery area. It shows repositories, collections, events, and recommendations beyond your immediate network.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should a screen reader user approach Explore?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use heading navigation to move through sections, and landmarks or lists where available. Explore pages can be content-dense, so structured navigation matters. Move by headings, then open items of interest rather than trying to read linearly through everything.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds like good advice for most complex web pages.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is.

speaker: ALEX
text: Trending is related but more time-sensitive. It highlights projects or developers that are popular right now.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And you can filter trending results?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, often by language and time period. There is also a view for trending developers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about CLI here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Trending repositories are not really exposed as a first-class official API feature in the same way some other data is. But you can approximate trending by searching for recently starred popular repositories, including within a specific language.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone wants trending Rust accessibility tools, they may need a mix of web exploration and clever search.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Topics are another discovery tool. Topics are category labels for repositories.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I browse a topic like accessibility or screen-reader, I can discover related repositories quickly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Browsing topics helps you move from one relevant project to many. Useful topics for this learning community might include accessibility, a11y, screen-reader, docs, education, open-source, python, javascript, vscode, assistive-technology, and beginner-friendly labels depending on the repo metadata.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And from the CLI side?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can search for repositories with a given topic, and if it's your own repository, you can add topics to improve discoverability.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That means topics are both a search tool and a contribution to the ecosystem.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Lists are for organizing your stars into collections.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So stars are the raw pile, and lists are the shelves.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You can create a list, add repositories to it, then view or share that list. It's useful when you want separate collections like accessibility tools, learning repositories, documentation examples, or projects to contribute to later.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you manage lists from the CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: Not in the same way. Lists are managed through the web interface. The CLI can help you view stars, but list management is web-based.

speaker: ALEX
text: One especially valuable part of this material is how to find accessible and inclusive projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Yes, because discovery is not neutral. If you know what to search for, you find healthier places to contribute.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Use search strategies that combine subject matter and contribution signals. Search for accessibility-focused repositories. Search for repositories with good first issues related to accessibility. Search for issues tagged both accessibility and help wanted.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of searching only by language, you search by values and workflow readiness.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Also follow organizations known for accessibility work, inclusive design, assistive technology, open education, and good documentation practices. And one smart social trick is to look at who trusted people in your community follow. That's often a better discovery path than trending alone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because it gives you a human-curated network instead of just popularity.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's connect all of this to building your own presence. Your contribution graph, pinned repositories, and profile README all help people understand your work. But the goal is not to perform productivity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I'm glad you said that.

speaker: ALEX
text: The real goal is clarity. Show what you care about. Pin repositories that represent your interests or contributions. Use your README to introduce yourself. Contribute steadily if you can, but don't treat the graph like a moral scorecard.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And activity tips?

speaker: ALEX
text: Small consistent contributions count. Issues, pull requests, discussions, documentation edits, and review participation all help you become visible in a healthy way. Focus on meaningful engagement, not gaming metrics.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before we close, let's gather the CLI angle on social features, because many of our learners like working from the terminal.

speaker: JAMIE
text: A quick tour then.

speaker: ALEX
text: For following: you can follow or unfollow users, list who you follow, list followers, and check follow relationships. For stars: you can star and unstar repositories, list your starred repositories, and check whether you've starred a particular repo. For discovery: some searching can be done through CLI, including repository search by topic or other qualifiers, though Explore and Lists are still mainly web experiences. For watching: core actions like watch, unwatch, ignore, and list watched repositories are available, but very granular watch settings may still require GitHub.com.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the CLI is strong for direct actions, but weaker for highly visual discovery surfaces.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a fair rule of thumb.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's end with a practical screen reader navigation recap across these social features. To follow a user, go to the profile and navigate by buttons until you reach Follow. To star a repository, use heading or landmark navigation to get to the repository header, then move to the Star button. To browse your stars, go to your profile or stars page and navigate through repository lists and headings. To explore topics, move by headings and links on the topics or search pages. And for Explore and Trending, don't try to consume the whole page linearly--use structure to jump between sections.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a really important meta-skill: on GitHub, navigation strategy is part of technical literacy.

speaker: ALEX
text: It absolutely is.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if we put the whole episode into one thought, maybe it's this: GitHub has social and structural features that shape how you discover projects, support maintainers, present your work, and participate in communities. Learning them makes you a more effective contributor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Beautifully said. And one final note: the authoritative details for these features can evolve, so GitHub Docs and the GitHub changelog remain your best reference points for up-to-date behavior.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Thanks for learning with us.

speaker: ALEX
text: See you next time.
